# Research Impact at Purdue

**Altmetrics Definition**

Altmetrics, or alternative metrics is a catch all term which refers to the statistical analysis of alternative forms of capture of research articles and research products such as presentations and conference proceedings. In other words, alt-metrics is all of the other ways of capturing and measuring how people use research output. Examples are twitter mentions, download counts, and comments on your work. Such metrics are imprecise, but they do capture the ways in which your and other scholars work is being used. Altmetricians look at work on the article level rather than at the journal or research field level, thus their work tells stories about an individual researcher rather than a body of researchers like bibliometrics does.

## Uses of Altmetrics

Uses of Altmetrics

## Types of Altmetrics Explained

Altmetrics appear at the article, poster, and conference proceedings level. Therefore the following breakdown is broken down by type

Article

Posters

Books

## Plum Analytics

![plum analytics logo](https://libapps.s3.amazonaws.com/accounts/389917/images/Untitled.jpg)

![plum analytics logo](https://libapps.s3.amazonaws.com/accounts/389917/images/Untitled.jpg)

Plum Analytics is a suite of products which focuses on altmetrics. It draws its the majority of its data from Amazon Books, Facebook, Twitter, Git-hub, Mendeley, and Scopus.

Plum Analytics uses a number of common alt-metrics such as usage, captures, and mentions.

# Altmetrics

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Locations_of_papers_in_a_map_of_science_and_locations_of_the_key_papers_for_Nobel_prizes.tif/lossy-page1-110px-Locations_of_papers_in_a_map_of_science_and_locations_of_the_key_papers_for_Nobel_prizes.tif.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Altmetrics.svg/250px-Altmetrics.svg.png)
![A ranking of five scholarly papers based on their Altmetric Attention Scores.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Ooir.png/250px-Ooir.png)

In scholarly and scientific publishing, **altmetrics** (stands for "alternative metrics") are non-traditional [bibliometrics](/wiki/Bibliometrics "Bibliometrics")[[2]](#cite_note-PLOSCollections-2) proposed as an alternative[[3]](#cite_note-3) or complement[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) to more traditional [citation impact](/wiki/Citation_impact "Citation impact") metrics, such as [impact factor](/wiki/Impact_factor "Impact factor") and [*h*-index](/wiki/H-index "H-index").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The term altmetrics was proposed in 2010,[[1]](#cite_note-Altmetrics-Manifesto-2011-1) as a generalization of [article level metrics](/wiki/Article_level_metrics "Article level metrics"),[[6]](#cite_note-UCBerkeley-BinfieldTalk-2009-6) and has its roots in the #altmetrics [hashtag](/wiki/Hashtag "Hashtag"). Although altmetrics are often thought of as metrics about articles, they can be applied to people, journals, books, data sets, presentations, videos, source code repositories, web pages, etc.

Altmetrics use public APIs across platforms to gather data with open scripts and algorithms. Altmetrics did not originally cover [citation](/wiki/Citation "Citation") counts,[[7]](#cite_note-OpeningScience-2014-7) but calculate scholar impact based on diverse online research output, such as social media, online news media, online reference managers and so on.[[8]](#cite_note-IEEESpectrum-MeasuringImpact-2012-8)[[9]](#cite_note-SerialsReview-Rethinking-2013-9) It demonstrates both the impact and the detailed composition of the impact.[[1]](#cite_note-Altmetrics-Manifesto-2011-1) Altmetrics could be applied to research filter,[[1]](#cite_note-Altmetrics-Manifesto-2011-1) promotion and tenure dossiers, grant applications[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) and for ranking newly-published articles in [academic search engines](/wiki/List_of_academic_databases_and_search_engines "List of academic databases and search engines").[[12]](#cite_note-:6-12)

Overtime, the diversity of sources mentioning, citing, or archiving articles has gone down. This happened because services ceased to exist, like Connotea, or because changes in API availability. For example, PlumX removed Twitter metrics in August 2023.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Adoption

The development of web 2.0 has changed the research publication seeking and sharing within or outside the academy, but also provides new innovative constructs to measure the broad scientific impact of scholar work. Although the traditional metrics are useful, they might be insufficient to measure immediate and uncited impacts, especially outside the peer-review realm.[[1]](#cite_note-Altmetrics-Manifesto-2011-1)

Projects such as [ImpactStory](/wiki/ImpactStory "ImpactStory"),[[14]](#cite_note-Insights-ImpactStory-2013-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Impactstory-About-15) and various companies, including [Altmetric](/wiki/Altmetric "Altmetric"),[[14]](#cite_note-Insights-ImpactStory-2013-14)[[16]](#cite_note-Altmetric-About-16) [Plum Analytics](/wiki/Plum_Analytics "Plum Analytics")[[14]](#cite_note-Insights-ImpactStory-2013-14)[[17]](#cite_note-JERML-PlumX-2016-17)[[18]](#cite_note-PlumAnalytics-About-18)[[19]](#cite_note-PlumAnalytics-AboutAltmetrics-19) and Overton[[20]](#cite_note-20) are calculating altmetrics. Several publishers have started providing such information to readers, including [BioMed Central](/wiki/BioMed_Central "BioMed Central"), [Public Library of Science (PLOS)](/wiki/PLOS "PLOS"),[[21]](#cite_note-PLoS-AltMetrics-2005-21)[[22]](#cite_note-PLOS-ArticleLevelMetrics-22) [Frontiers](/wiki/Frontiers_(publisher) "Frontiers (publisher)"),[[23]](#cite_note-Frontiers-About-23) [Nature Publishing Group](/wiki/Nature_Publishing_Group "Nature Publishing Group"),[[24]](#cite_note-Nature-Altmetrics-2012-24) and [Elsevier](/wiki/Elsevier "Elsevier").[[25]](#cite_note-MarketWatch-Elsevier-2012-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Scopus-NewArticleMetrics-2015-26) The [NIHR](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Health_and_Care_Research "National Institute for Health and Care Research") Journals Library also includes altmetric data alongside its publications.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In 2008, the [Journal of Medical Internet Research](/wiki/Journal_of_Medical_Internet_Research "Journal of Medical Internet Research") started to systematically collect tweets about its articles.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28) Starting in March 2009, the [Public Library of Science](/wiki/Public_Library_of_Science "Public Library of Science") also introduced article-level metrics for all articles.[[21]](#cite_note-PLoS-AltMetrics-2005-21)[[22]](#cite_note-PLOS-ArticleLevelMetrics-22)[[29]](#cite_note-Lagotto-PLOS-29) Funders have started showing interest in alternative metrics,[[30]](#cite_note-Nature-Piwowar-Altmetrics-2013-30) including the UK Medical Research Council.[[31]](#cite_note-Nature-Viney-Altmetrics-2013-31) Altmetrics have been used in applications for promotion review by researchers.[[32]](#cite_note-Nature-Kwok-Altmetrics-2013-32) Furthermore, several universities, including the [University of Pittsburgh](/wiki/University_of_Pittsburgh "University of Pittsburgh") are experimenting with altmetrics at an institute level.[[32]](#cite_note-Nature-Kwok-Altmetrics-2013-32)

However, it is also observed that an article needs little attention to jump to the [upper quartile](/wiki/Quartile "Quartile") of ranked papers,[[33]](#cite_note-Infiniflux-AltmetricRankings-2013-33) suggesting that not enough sources of altmetrics are currently available to give a balanced picture of impact for the majority of papers.

Important in determining the relative impact of a paper, a service that calculates altmetrics statistics needs a considerably sized knowledge base. The following table shows the number of artefacts, including papers, covered by services:

| Website | Number of artefacts |
| --- | --- |
| Plum Analytics | ~ 52.6 million[[34]](#cite_note-PlumCoverage-34) |
| Altmetric.com | ~ 28 million[[35]](#cite_note-35) |
| ImpactStory | ~ 1 million[[36]](#cite_note-Twitter-Impactstory-Tracking-2016-36) |
| Overton | ~ 11 million[[37]](#cite_note-37) |

## Categories

Altmetrics are a very broad group of metrics, capturing various parts of impact a paper or work can have. A classification of altmetrics was proposed by ImpactStory in September 2012,[[38]](#cite_note-NewFramework-38) and a very similar classification is used by the Public Library of Science:[[39]](#cite_note-Lin2013-39)

### Viewed

One of the first alternative metrics to be used was the number of views of a paper. Traditionally, an author would wish to publish in a journal with a high subscription rate, so many people would have access to the research. With the introduction of web technologies it became possible to actually count how often a single paper was looked at. Typically, publishers count the number of HTML views and PDF views. As early as 2004, the *[BMJ](/wiki/BMJ "BMJ")* published the number of views for its articles, which was found to be somewhat correlated to citations.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Discussed

The discussion of a paper can be seen as a metric that captures the potential impact of a paper. Typical sources of data to calculate this metric include [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), [Google+](/wiki/Google%2B "Google+"), [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), Science Blogs, and Wikipedia pages. Some researchers regard the mentions on social media as citations. For example, citations on a social media platform could be divided into two categories: internal and external. For instance, the former includes retweets, the latter refers to tweets containing links to outside documents.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The correlation between the mentions and likes and citation by primary scientific literature has been studied, and a slight correlation at best was found, e.g. for articles in [PubMed](/wiki/PubMed "PubMed").[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

In 2008 the *[Journal of Medical Internet Research](/wiki/Journal_of_Medical_Internet_Research "Journal of Medical Internet Research")* began publishing views and [tweets](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"). These "tweetations" proved to be a good indicator of highly cited articles, leading the author to propose a "Twimpact factor", which is the number of Tweets it receives in the first seven days of publication, as well as a Twindex, which is the rank percentile of an article's Twimpact factor.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28) However, if implementing use of the Twimpact factor, research shows scores to be highly subject specific, and as a result, comparisons of Twimpact factors should be made between papers of the same subject area.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28) While past research in the literature has demonstrated a correlation between tweetations and citations, it is not a causative relationship. At this point in time, it is unclear whether higher citations occur as a result of greater media attention via Twitter and other platforms, or is simply reflective of the quality of the article itself.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28)

Recent research conducted at the individual level, rather than the article level, supports the use of Twitter and social media platforms as a mechanism for increasing impact value.[[43]](#cite_note-:3-43) Results indicate that researchers whose work is mentioned on Twitter have significantly higher h-indices than those of researchers whose work was not mentioned on Twitter. The study highlights the role of using discussion based platforms, such as Twitter, in order to increase the value of traditional impact metrics.

Besides Twitter and other streams, blogging has shown to be a powerful platform to discuss literature. Various platforms exist that keep track of which papers are being blogged about. Altmetric.com uses this information for calculating metrics, while other tools just report where discussion is happening, such as ResearchBlogging and Chemical blogspace.

### Recommended

Platforms may even provide a formal way of ranking papers or recommending papers otherwise, such as [Faculty of 1000](/wiki/Faculty_of_1000 "Faculty of 1000").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Saved

It is also informative to quantify the number of times a page has been saved, or bookmarked. It is thought that individuals typically choose to bookmark pages that have a high relevance to their own work, and as a result, bookmarks may be an additional indicator of impact for a specific study. Providers of such information include science specific [social bookmarking](/wiki/Social_bookmarking "Social bookmarking") services such as [CiteULike](/wiki/CiteULike "CiteULike") and [Mendeley](/wiki/Mendeley "Mendeley").

### Cited

The cited category is a narrowed definition, different from the discussion. Besides the traditional metrics based on citations in scientific literature, such as those obtained from [Google Scholar](/wiki/Google_Scholar "Google Scholar"), [CrossRef](/wiki/CrossRef "CrossRef"), [PubMed Central](/wiki/PubMed_Central "PubMed Central"), and [Scopus](/wiki/Scopus "Scopus"), altmetrics also adopt citations in secondary knowledge sources. For example, ImpactStory counts the number of times a paper has been referenced by Wikipedia.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Plum Analytics also provides metrics for various academic publications,[[46]](#cite_note-:1-46) seeking to track research productivity. PLOS is also a tool that may be used to utilize information on engagement.[[46]](#cite_note-:1-46)

Numerous studies have shown that scientific articles disseminated through social media channels (i.e. Twitter, Reddit, Facebook, YouTube, etc) have substantially higher biblometric scores (downlodas, reads and citations) than articles not advertised through social media. In the fields of plastic surgery,[[47]](#cite_note-47) hand surgery[[48]](#cite_note-48) and more, higher Altmetric scores are associated with better short-term bibliometrics.

## Interpretation

While there is less consensus on the validity and consistency of altmetrics,[[49]](#cite_note-49) the interpretation of altmetrics in particular is discussed. Proponents of altmetrics make clear that many of the metrics show attention or engagement, rather than the quality of impacts on the progress of science.[[39]](#cite_note-Lin2013-39) Even citation-based metrics do not indicate if a high score implies a positive impact on science; that is, papers are also cited in papers that disagree with the cited paper, an issue for example addressed by the Citation Typing Ontology project.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Altmetrics could be more appropriately interpreted by providing detailed context and qualitative data. For example, in order to evaluate the scientific contribution of a scholar work to policy making by altmetrics, qualitative data, such as who's citing online[[12]](#cite_note-:6-12) and to what extent the online citation is relevant to the policymaking, should be provided as evidence.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Regarding the relatively low correlation between traditional metrics and altmetrics, altmetrics might measure complementary perspectives of the scholar impact. It is reasonable to combine and compare the two types of metrics in interpreting the societal and scientific impacts. Researchers built a 2\*2 framework based on the interactions between altmetrics and traditional citations.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) Further explanations should be provided for the two groups with high altmetrics/low citations and low altmetrics/high citations.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) Thus, altmetrics provide convenient approaches for researchers and institutions to monitor the impact of their work and avoid inappropriate interpretations.

## Controversy

The usefulness of metrics for estimating scientific impact is controversial.[[52]](#cite_note-:63-52)[[53]](#cite_note-:4-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-:7-55) Research has found that online buzz could amplify the effect of other forms of outreach on researchers' scientific impact. For the nano-scientists that are mentioned on Twitter, their interactions with reporters and non-scientists positively and significantly predicted higher h-index, whereas the non-mentioned group failed.[[43]](#cite_note-:3-43) Altmetrics expands the measurement of scholar impact for containing a rapid uptake, a broader range of audiences and diverse research outputs. In addition, the community shows a clear need: funders demand measurables on the impact of their spending, such as public engagement.

However, there are limitations that affect the usefulness due to technique problems and systematic bias of construct, such as data quality, heterogeneity and particular dependencies.[[53]](#cite_note-:4-53) In terms of technique problems, the data might be incomplete, because it is difficult to collect those online research outputs without direct links to their mentions (i.e. videos) and identify different versions of one research work. Additionally, whether the API leads to any missing data is unsolved.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

As for systematic bias, like other metrics, altmetrics are prone to self-citation, gaming, and other mechanisms to boost one's apparent impact such as performing [citation spam](/wiki/Wikipedia:Spam#Citation_spam "Wikipedia:Spam") in Wikipedia. Altmetrics can be [gamed](/wiki/Gaming_the_system "Gaming the system"): for example, likes and mentions can be bought.[[56]](#cite_note-Beall2013-56) Altmetrics can be more difficult to standardize than citations. One example is the number of tweets linking to a paper where the number can vary widely depending on how the tweets are collected.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Besides, online popularity may not equal to scientific values. Some popular online citations might be far from the value of generating further research discoveries, while some theoretical-driven or minority-targeted research of great science-related importance might be marginalized online.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28) For example, the top tweeted articles in biomedicine in 2011 were relevant to curious or funny content, potential health applications, and catastrophe.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) [Altmetric](/wiki/Altmetric "Altmetric") state that they have systems in place to detect, identify and correct gaming.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Finally, recent research has shown Altmetrics reproduce gendered biases found in disciplinary publication and citation practices: for example, journal articles authored exclusively by female scholars score 27% lower on average than exclusively male-authored outputs. At once, this same research shows 0 attention scores are more likely for male-authored articles.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Altmetrics for more recent articles may be higher because of the increasing uptake of the social web and because articles may be mentioned mainly when they are published.[[60]](#cite_note-:5-60) As a result, it might not be fair to compare the altmetrics scores of articles unless they have been published at a similar time. Researchers has developed a sign test to avoid the usage uptake bias by comparing the metrics of an article with the two articles published immediately before and after it.[[60]](#cite_note-:5-60)

It should be kept in mind that the metrics are only one of the outcomes of tracking how research is disseminated and used. Altmetrics should be carefully interpreted to overcome the bias. Even more informative than knowing how often a paper is cited, is which papers are citing it. That information allows researchers to see how their work is impacting the field (or not). Providers of metrics also typically provide access to the information from which the metrics were calculated. For example, [Web of Science](/wiki/Web_of_Science "Web of Science") shows which are the citing papers, ImpactStory shows which Wikipedia pages are referencing the paper, and CitedIn shows which databases extracted data from the paper.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Another concern of altmetrics, or any metrics, is how universities or institutions are using metrics to rank their employees make promotion or funding decisions,[[62]](#cite_note-62) and the aim should be limited to measure engagement.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

The overall online research output is very little and varied among different disciplines.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) The phenomenon might be consistent with the social media use among scientists. Surveys has shown that nearly half of their respondents held ambivalent attitudes of social media's influence on academic impact and never announced their research work on social media.[[64]](#cite_note-64) With the changing shift in open science and social media use, the consistent altmetrics across disciplines and institutions will more likely be adopted.

## Ongoing research

The specific use cases and characteristics is an active research field in [bibliometrics](/wiki/Bibliometrics "Bibliometrics"), providing much needed data to measure the impact of altmetrics itself. Public Library of Science has an Altmetrics Collection[[65]](#cite_note-PLosONE-AltmetricsCollection-2012-65) and both the *Information Standards Quarterly* and the *Aslib Journal of Information Management* recently published special issues on altmetrics.[[66]](#cite_note-NISO-InfoStandardsQuarterly-66)[[67]](#cite_note-Aslib-2015-67) A series of articles that extensively reviews altmetrics was published in late 2015.[[68]](#cite_note-WebIndicators-Part1-2016-68)[[69]](#cite_note-WebIndicators-Part2-2016-69)[[70]](#cite_note-WebIndicators-Part3-2016-70)

There is other research examining the validity of one altmetrics[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28) or make comparisons across different platforms.[[60]](#cite_note-:5-60) Researchers examine the correlation between altmetrics and traditional citations as the validity test. They assume that the positive and significant correlation reveals the accuracy of altmetrics to measure scientific impact as citations.[[60]](#cite_note-:5-60) The low correlation (less than 0.30[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)) leads to the conclusion that altmetrics serves a complementary role in scholar impact measurement such as the study by Lamba (2020) [[71]](#cite_note-71) who examined 2343 articles having both altmetric attention scores and citations published by 22 core health care policy faculty members at Harvard Medical School and a significant strong positive correlation (r>0.4) was observed between the aggregated ranked altmetric attention scores and ranked citation/increased citation values for all the faculty members in the study. However, it remains unsolved that what altmetrics are most valuable and what degree of correlation between two metrics generates a stronger impact on the measurement. Additionally, the validity test itself faces some technical problems as well. For example, replication of the data collection is impossible because of the instant changing algorithms of data providers.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

# What are altmetrics?

Track meaningful engagement with research as it is shared, mentioned, reviewed, and read online.

![a question mark](https://wordpress-uploads-production.s3.amazonaws.com/uploads/2022/10/What-are-altmetrics-2.svg)

## [Evidence research influence](#evidence-your-influence)

**Instantly trace the reach of research beyond traditional bibliometrics**

Altmetrics are a simple and effective way of understanding who is engaging with research online and what they’re saying. The Altmetric data gathered allows you to track anything from articles to software packages, using a broad range of scholarly identifiers that cut out the noise of traditional measurement tools.

Use altmetrics to quickly collate a record of relevant online attention, with less time spent sifting through data. Positive or negative sentiment can be evidenced, and you can even demonstrate when research makes tangible changes to a field of study.

Our tools and services cater to many different communities involved in the production of research, and customizable interactive dashboards allow each team to access the information most relevant to them.

## [Benefits and applications](#benefits-and-applications)

--- SOURCE 4: What are Altmetrics? - Altmetrics - Guides at University of Pittsburgh ---
URL: https://pitt.libguides.com/altmetrics

SUMMARY:
Defined. Altmetrics let us measure and monitor the reach and impact of scholarship and research through online interactions.

FULL CONTENT:
![University of Pittsburgh Library System](https://library.pitt.edu/themes/custom/uls2020/images/ULS_logo1.png "University of Pittsburgh Library System")

# University of Pittsburgh Library System

## Course & Subject Guides

# Altmetrics

![Profile Photo](//d2jv02qf7xgjwx.cloudfront.net/accounts/115188/profiles/107080/Head_shot.jpg)

## Introduction to altmetrics

# What are altmetrics?

Altmetrics expand our view of what impact looks like, but also of what’s making the impact. This matters because expressions of scholarship are becoming more diverse.

from <http://altmetrics.org/manifesto/>

## Mental Status imageDefined

![Mental Status image](http://s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/11508/images/mental_status-128.png)

Altmetrics let us measure and monitor the reach and impact of scholarship and research through online interactions. Altmetrics stands for "alternative metrics." The "alternative" part references traditional measurements of academic success such as citation counts, journal prestige (impact factor), and author H-index. Altmetrics are meant to complement, not totally replace, these traditional measures.

Supporters of the altmetrics movement believe that doing so will give a more complete picture of how research and scholarship is used.

Simply, altmetrics are metrics beyond traditional citations. 

## Why they matterGroups image

![Groups image](http://s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/11508/images/groups-128.png)

There is increasing understanding that scholarly research has moved beyond the printed page and that traditional measures of impact are inadequate. Citations are only a small part of the scholarly ecosystem and only represent one type of impact. Other media types of increasing importance such as data, tools, software, websites, videos, etc. produced for or during the research process may be just as, or more, important than the articles that accompany them.

Since most research, including journal articles, are now electronic and networked we can track how many times they are accessed, used, and shared. These numbers provide a more complete picture of the reach and impact of research and scholarship; one that goes beyond citations in peer-reviewed publications.

## Active Directory imageHow they work

![Active Directory image](http://s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/11508/images/active_directory-128.png)

You probably already know that nearly everything on the internet is tracked. What you click can be used to inform website design, serve targeted adds, or as a simple measure of popularity. Altmetrics uses this ability to track interaction with online items as a way of measuring research impact and reach.

Altmetrics can answer questions such as:

## Altmetrics by item type

## Benefits of Altmetrics

**![social connections](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Conference-52.png)Capture elements of societal impact**  
Altmetrics data can inform researchers of elements of the societal impact of their research. For example, altmetrics data can help researchers understand how their research is being interacted with by the public, government, policy makers, and other researchers.

![social connections](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Conference-52.png)

**![networking map](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Mind_Map-52.png)Complement traditional metrics**  
Altmetrics provide a wider range of data, from a wider range of sources than traditional metrics. Altmetrics data is also highly nuanced and can be provided in high detail and in the context in which it originates.

![networking map](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Mind_Map-52.png)

**![rocket ship taking off](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Rocket-52.png)Offer speed and discoverability**  
Altmetrics data accumulates at a faster speed compared to traditional metrics. In disciplines where citations grow slowly, or in the context of new researchers, this speed helps determine which outputs are gaining online attention.

![rocket ship taking off](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Rocket-52.png)

**![an open padlock](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Unlock_2-52.png)Open access advantage**  
Providers like Altmetric.com and ImpactStory provide access to their API and source code. Altmetrics providers also pull their data from open sources, who give access to their APIs or raw usage data, which makes altmetrics data more easily replicable than data in proprietary databases.

![an open padlock](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Unlock_2-52.png)

## Considerations for Altmetrics

Altmetrics offer a lot of information. Here are some considerations to keep in mind when using and analyzing altmetrics data.

**![checklist](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Report_Card-52.png)​Altmetrics lack a standard definition**  
The field of altmetrics remains undecided on what altmetrics truly measure. However, the [NISO Alternative Assessment Metrics (Altmetrics) Initiative](http://www.niso.org/topics/tl/altmetrics_initiative/) is currently working to create a standard definition of the term and has a draft of its definition open for public comment.

![checklist](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Report_Card-52.png)

**![even level bar graph](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Neutral_Trading-52.png)Altmetrics data are not normalized**  
It is not advised to compare between sources and data sets for altmetrics, as different providers collect different kinds of data. Instead, we suggest using altmetrics to tell a story about your research - see the "Use Cases" tab for more information.  

![even level bar graph](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Neutral_Trading-52.png)

**![calendar with a timer](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Overtime-52.png)​Almetrics are time-dependent**  
Altmetrics provide information about the use of the work, but much of this use has a lifespan - and that lifespan is unknown. For older works, there may not be much altmetrics activity, but that does not necessarily mean that the work is not heavily used!

![calendar with a timer](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Overtime-52.png)

**![many inputs to one document](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Multiple_Inputs-52.png)​Altmetrics have known tracking issues**  
Altmetrics work best with items that have a Digital Object Identifier (DOI). PlumX is one provider that can track usage of an item with only a URL, but not all providers provide the same level of tracking for items without DOIs.

![many inputs to one document](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/Multiple_Inputs-52.png)

## What can Altmetrics do for me?

**![ four researchers analyzing a document.](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/meeting_stockphoto.jpg)**

![ four researchers analyzing a document.](//s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/accounts/22857/images/meeting_stockphoto.jpg)

SUMMARY:
The Altmetric score represents a weighted count of the amount of attention for a research output from a variety of sources.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/researchevaluationpanelaltmetrics1-160124184729/85/OpenCon-Research-Evaluation-Panel-Altmetrics-2-320.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A slide titled "Summary" outlines key points about altmetrics, including their definition, significance in research evaluation, and the relationships between altmetrics and openness.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/researchevaluationpanelaltmetrics1-160124184729/85/OpenCon-Research-Evaluation-Panel-Altmetrics-3-320.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image presents a definition of altmetrics, explaining it as a field focused on creating and studying new metrics derived from social media to evaluate research impact, with references to key sources by M. Thelwall and I. Maflahi.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/researchevaluationpanelaltmetrics1-160124184729/85/OpenCon-Research-Evaluation-Panel-Altmetrics-5-320.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image features logos of altmetric providers—Altmetric, Impactstory, PLOS, and Plum Analytics—along with a brief description highlighting their role in providing output-level metrics from diverse sources, including traditional citations and social media, for research evaluation.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/researchevaluationpanelaltmetrics1-160124184729/85/OpenCon-Research-Evaluation-Panel-Altmetrics-7-320.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image depicts a slide titled "Altmetrics and Research Evaluation: Reality," highlighting that some funding agencies use altmetrics to support their strategies, mentioning sources like the Centre Trust (C. Dinsmore, L. Allen, K. Dolly, 2014) and noting challenges such as the lack of consistent identifiers and the need for tools like those developed by Depsy to track research software impact.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://images.slideplayer.com/31/9645480/slides/slide_2.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image lists various types of altmetrics in research evaluation, including social media impact, heterogeneity with examples like reader counts from Mendeley, suggestions, blog mentions, microblogging on Twitter, and emphasizes the need to differentiate and address the lack of clear meaning and theoretical foundations.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Altmetrics, or alternative metrics, refer to non-traditional metrics that capture the online engagement and impact of research outputs beyond conventional citation counts.

These measures include social media mentions, downloads, and discussions, showcasing the diverse ways research interacts with broader audiences, thus providing real-time insights into research impact.
Altmetrics evaluate societal impact by tracking public engagement with research, including mentions by policymakers and the media, highlighting research relevance beyond academia.

Altmetrics complement traditional metrics like citation counts and impact factors, offering a more comprehensive understanding of research influence and dissemination in both academic and public spheres.
